rationale: Maternal viral infection drives sustained fetal neuroinflammation via maternal cytokine surge (IL-6, IL-17a). MIA model is the most studied prenatal autism risk pathway.
MIA produces persistent microglial priming in offspring; replicated in poly(I:C) and LPS rodent models.
Maternal mast-cell / histaminergic activation contributes to BBB permeability changes downstream of MIA.
Children exposed to first-trimester maternal infection show elevated regression rates and immune-inflammatory phenotype markers.
SHANK3, NLGN, NRXN, FMR1 — the SFARI Tier-1 synaptic genes — converge on synaptic pruning/maturation pathways.
Polygenic risk converges on E/I balance via SCN2A, GRIN2B, GABRB3 channelopathies.
TSC1, TSC2, PTEN syndromic mutations confirm mTOR is a major convergence point of monogenic and polygenic ASD risk.
Polygenic risk enriched in mTOR-pathway syndromic phenotype (TSC, PTEN macrocephaly).
FMR1 is the canonical polygenic-→-syndromic bridge.
Rapamycin (mTOR inhibitor) → FOXO disinhibition. Rapamycin's neuroprotective profile in autism syndromic models partially operates through FOXO-driven autophagy.
Resveratrol activates SIRT1 → SIRT1 deacetylates FOXO3a → activates FOXO antioxidant + autophagy program.
Alpha-lipoic acid restores insulin sensitivity → modulates AKT-FOXO axis under conditions of insulin resistance.
Aerobic exercise activates AMPK → AMPK directly phosphorylates FOXO3 → promotes mitochondrial biogenesis and autophagy.
Lithium inhibits GSK-3β → stabilizes β-catenin → upregulates BDNF expression. Well-characterized neurotrophic mechanism.
GSK-3β cross-talks with mTOR (TSC2 phosphorylation); lithium thus indirectly modulates mTOR pathway activity.
Myo-inositol is the precursor for phosphoinositides (PIP2, PIP3) that underlie PI3K/AKT signaling.
Inositol depletion has been linked to serotonergic/repetitive-behavior circuits via PI cycle exhaustion (lithium hypothesis).
Glycine is an obligate co-agonist at the NMDA receptor glycine site; directly modulates calcium/glutamate-NMDA homeostasis.
Glycine is one of three amino acid precursors of glutathione (γ-Glu-Cys-Gly); supplies oxidative-stress defense substrate.
Removes gliadin/casomorphin opioid peptides; modulates gut-brain axis. RCT evidence for whole-population is null/mixed; effect concentrated in GI/microbiome-phenotype subset.
Reduces intestinal permeability / LPS translocation in zonulin-elevated subset.
Responder phenotype is the GI/microbiome subgroup specifically.
UVB-driven cutaneous vitamin D synthesis is the principal natural source; vitamin D regulates TPH2 / brain serotonin synthesis.
Morning sunlight entrains the suprachiasmatic nucleus and normalizes the HPA cortisol awakening response.
